1: Symeon Petros — the Hebrew/Aramaic form of Simon (cf. Acts 15:14), more formal and Semitic than the common Greek Simōn; the double name may signal the letter's claim of apostolic authenticity. doulos kai apostolos — slave and envoy: the two terms bracket the range from complete dependence to delegated authority. tois isotimon hēmin lachousin pistin — to those who have obtained a faith equally precious with ours (isotimon = equal in honor/value; lagchanō = to receive by lot, suggesting divine allocation rather than human achievement).
2: epignōsis — full/thorough knowledge (the epi-strengthened form of gnōsis; epi + ginōskō = to know fully, recognize completely). This word appears throughout 2 Peter (2 Peter 1:2, 3, 8; 2 Peter 2:20; 2 Peter 3:18) as the letter's central epistemological term — placed in direct contrast to the false teachers' claims to gnōsis. Grace and peace multiply en epignōsei — through the knowledge of God and of Jesus; right knowledge of God is the channel of covenant blessing.
3: tēs theias dynameōs autou — his divine power (the theia dynamis = divine power, a phrase common in Hellenistic philosophy and Jewish Wisdom literature for God's operative energy); ta pros zōēn kai eusebeian — everything pertaining to life and godliness (eusebeia = godliness, reverence — a distinctly Greek religious term that 2 Peter uses 4 times; rare in Paul but common in the Pastoral Epistles). dia epignōseōs tou kalesantos hēmas — through the knowledge of the one who called us; the calling is by the divine doxē kai aretē (glory and virtue/excellence — the two defining qualities of divine being in Greek philosophical theology).
4: theias koinōnoi physeōs — partakers/sharers of the divine nature (this phrase is unique in the NT; koinōnoi physeōs uses the language of Stoic and Middle Platonic philosophy about participation in the divine principle that pervades reality). Peter applies it to the new covenant: believers share in the divine nature not by absorption into God (the Platonic divinization trajectory) but by escaping the phthora (corruption/decay) that pervades the created order. phygontes tēs en tō kosmō en epithymia phthoras — having escaped the corruption that is in the world through desire; the two-stage movement: escape from the old creation's decay and positive participation in the divine life.
5: kai auto touto de spoudēn pasan pareisenenkontes — and for this very reason, bringing alongside all diligence/effort (spoudē = earnest effort; pareispheromai = to bring in alongside — a compound of motion with co-participation). The virtue chain begins: episychorigēsate en tē pistei hymōn tēn aretēn — supply in your faith virtue (epichorēgeō = to fund/supply in addition; the word for the wealthy patron who supplements the chorus's resources with his own). The chain is a sorites (chain argument) or climax (ladder figure) — a Hellenistic rhetorical structure for progressive moral development: pistis (faith) → aretē (virtue/excellence).
6: en de tē aretē tēn gnōsin — in virtue, knowledge; en de tē gnōsei tēn enkrateian — in knowledge, self-control (enkrateia = mastery over oneself; the Stoic cardinal virtue of temperance); en de tē enkrateia tēn hypomonēn — in self-control, endurance (hypomonē = the capacity to remain under pressure without breaking — the same virtue James prizes). The chain deliberately places gnōsis within a framework of practical virtue — a rebuke of those who claim knowledge while lacking the moral qualities that should accompany it.
7: en de tē hypomonē tēn eusebeian — in endurance, godliness; en de tē eusebeia tēn philadelphian — in godliness, brotherly/sisterly love (philadelphia = love of siblings/fellow believers); en de tē philadelphia tēn agapēn — in brotherly love, love (agapē). The chain ends with agapē — the comprehensive love that orients toward the other's good without regard to reciprocity. The movement of the chain: from individual faith through communal affection to universal love — each virtue is the foundation for the next.
8: tauta gar hymin hyparchonta kai pleonazonta — for these things being present in you and increasing (pleonazō = to increase, abound — the same root as pleon, more). ouk argous oude akarpous kathistēsin — they make you neither idle/barren nor unfruitful (argos = not-working, inactive — the failure-state that contradicts the virtue-chain; akarpos = without fruit). eis tēn tou Kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou epignōsin — with respect to the full knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ; the chain's telos is deeper knowledge of Christ, not mere ethical achievement.
9: hō de mē parestin tauta, typhlos estin, myōpazōn — the one for whom these things are absent is blind, short-sighted/squinting (myōpazōn = squinting, shortsighted — the medical term for myopia; this is the only NT occurrence of the word; blind spiritually sees nothing; short-sighted sees close-up immediate pleasures but misses the eschatological horizon). lēthēn labōn tou katharismou tōn palai autou hamartiōn — having received forgetfulness of his past-sins cleansing (lēthē = forgetfulness, oblivion — the mythological river Lethe; the person without virtues has forgotten they were cleansed at baptism).
10: spoudasate bebaian hymōn tēn klēsin kai eklogēn poiēsasthai — make every effort to make your calling and election firm (bebaian = firm, secure, reliable — a legal/commercial term for a guaranteed security; the calling and election belong to God but their bebaiotēs in the believer's lived experience is confirmed through the virtue-chain of 2 Peter 1:5-7). ou mē ptaisēte pote — you will by no means stumble at any time (the double negative ou mē is emphatic).
11: plousios gar epichōrēgēthēsetai hymin hē eisodos — for richly will be supplied to you the entrance (plousios = richly/abundantly; epichorēgeō = supply bountifully, the same generous funding word as 2 Peter 1:5). eis tēn aiōnion basileian — into the eternal kingdom of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. The contrast with the barely-saved (1 Peter 4:18) is implicit: the virtue-chain produces a rich entrance, not a narrow escape.
12: hypomimnēskein hymas aei peri toutōn — to keep reminding you always of these things (hypomimnēskō = to remind, bring to mind; the letter as an instrument of memory against forgetting, 2 Peter 1:9). kaitoige eidotas kai estērigmenous — although you know them and are established in the present truth (stērizō = to establish, firm up — the same root as stērigmos in 2 Peter 3:17).
13: en toutō tō skēnōmati — in this tent/tabernacle (the body as a temporary dwelling; skēnōma = a tent/booth — the same imagery as 2 Corinthians 5:1-4 where Paul describes the body as an earthly tent; also echoing John 1:14's skēnoō = to pitch a tent). diegeirein hymas en hypomnēsei — to stir you up by way of reminder (diegeirō = to arouse completely — a strong word for awakening from sleep/drowsiness).
14: hoti tacheinē estin hē apothesis tou skēnōmatos mou — that the putting-off of my tent is soon (apothesis = laying aside, putting off — the same clothing-removal word; death is the putting off of the tent-body). kathōs kai ho Kyrios hēmōn Iēsous Christos edēlōsen moi — as also our Lord Jesus Christ made clear to me (dēloō = to make plain, show; the reference may be to John 21:18-19 where Christ predicted Peter's death).
15: spoudasō de kai hekastote echein hymas — I will make every effort that you also may always have (spoudazō = to make haste, be diligent — the same root as spoudē in 2 Peter 1:5). meta tēn emēn exodon — after my departure/exodus (exodos = going out; the same word used of Moses' Exodus and of Jesus' "departure" at the Transfiguration in Luke 9:31 — deliberately chosen; Peter's death is his own exodus-event). The letter serves as a permanent testamentary reminder after its author's death.
16: ou gar sesophismenois mythois exakolouthēsantes — for not having followed cleverly devised/sophisticated myths (sesophismenois = perfect passive participle of sophizō = to make wise, craft cleverly; the false teachers' narratives are mythoi — myths/invented stories, in deliberate contrast to what Peter is about to assert). parousia — the coming, presence, arrival (the technical eschatological term). epoptai genēthentes — having become eyewitnesses (epoptēs = one who has seen with their own eyes; the technical term for initiates admitted to the highest level of the Eleusinian Mysteries who had seen the sacred objects — Peter applies the mysteries-vocabulary to the Transfiguration as the supreme revelation-event).
17: phōnēs ... hypo tēs megaloprepous doxēs — voice from the Majestic/Magnificent Glory (megaloprepēs = magnificent, befitting greatness — used as a divine title only here in the NT; the megaloprepous doxēs is the divine presence-cloud that spoke). ho Huios mou ho agapētos mou houtos estin, eis hon egō eudokēsa — This is my Son, my Beloved, in whom I have found delight (combining the Synoptic Transfiguration voice and the Baptism voice; eudokeō = to be well-pleased, to find one's delight in).
18: kai tautēn tēn phōnēn hēmeis ēkousamen ex ouranou enechtheisan — and this voice we ourselves heard borne from heaven (enenchthen = being carried — passive of pherō; same root as 2 Peter 1:21's pheromenoi for the prophets being borne by the Spirit). syn autō ontes en tō hagiō orei — being with him on the holy mountain; the Transfiguration as the founding event of Peter's apostolic knowledge, placed above the written prophetic word in terms of experiential priority (though the prophetic word is "more reliable" in 2 Peter 1:19 because it is more widely accessible).
19: ton prophētikon logon bebaiōteron — the prophetic word made more reliable (bebaiōteron = more firm, more reliable — the comparative of bebaios; the eyewitness experience confirms what the prophets wrote). lychnō phainonti en auchmērō topō — a lamp shining in a murky/squalid place (auchmēros = dark and murky, dry and dirty — the condition of the age before the eschatological dawn). heōsphoros — the morning star, Phosphorus (the planet Venus as the dawn-herald; the word combines heōs = dawn + pherō = to bear/bring; Christ is the dawn that ends the darkness; Revelation 22:16 calls him "the bright morning star").
20: idias epiluseōs — one's own interpretation/loosing (epilysis = unloosing, solving, interpreting — a word for solving riddles or unlocking enigmas; the verb epilyō means to unloose, explain). pāsa prophēteia graphēs — every prophecy of Scripture (the graphē — the written Scripture — as the corpus within which prophecy operates; no prophecy of Scripture is a matter of the prophet's own idiosyncratic interpretation). The verse grounds the authority of Scripture in its origin (2 Peter 1:21) rather than any human interpreter's cleverness.
21: ou gar thelēmati anthrōpou ēnechthē prophēteia pote — for no prophecy was ever brought by human will (pherō = to bear/bring/carry — the same verb as in 2 Peter 1:18; the prophecy was carried, not originated by human will). hypo Pneumatos hagiou pheromenoi elalēsan apo Theou anthrōpoi — carried along by the Holy Spirit, human beings spoke from God (pheromenoi = being borne along — the image of a ship carried by the wind; the prophets as vessels passively borne by the Spirit's wind while actively speaking). The two phrases contrast: human will drove the false teachers; the Spirit drove the true prophets.
